{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国}} and {{ill|Sōshū|ja|総州}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nihon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>

Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja}}</ref>

[[Category:Edo period Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo transportation history]] [[Category:Ferries in Japan]] [[Category:Water transport]] [[Category:Arakawa River system (Kantō)]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Sumida River Ferries]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sumida River Ferries]][[category:original pages]] [[ja:隅田川の渡し]]

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国}} and {{ill|Sōshū|ja|総州}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nihon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja}}</ref>

[[Category:Edo period Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo transportation history]] [[Category:Ferries in Japan]] [[Category:Water transport]] [[Category:Arakawa River system (Kantō)]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Sumida River Ferries]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sumida River Ferries]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Sumida River Ferries]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Sumida River Ferries]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sumida River Ferries]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sumida River Ferries]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Sumida River Ferries]][[Category:pp]] [[ja:隅田川の渡し]]

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nihon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nihon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. === Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nihon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>

Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nihon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

revision.comment: Created page with "{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]] '''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropo..."
revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} - '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)
{{Sumida River Bridges}} {{commonscat|Suijin Great Bridge}} {{Coord|35|44|10|N|139|48|51|E|display=title|region:JP_type:landmark}}

revision.text:
{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>

Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

revision.text:
{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the opposite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

revision.text:
{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the oqqosite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the oqqosite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国|en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the oqqosite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the o11osite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. == Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province |en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the o11osite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>

Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the o11osite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

{{ill|Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|The Ferry at Fujimi|ja|葛飾北斎|en}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|冨嶽三十六景|en}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|en}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province |en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the o11osite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province |en}} and {{ill|総州|lt=Sōshū|ja|総州|en}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|en}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|en}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|automobiles|ja|自動車|en}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|ja|日本化薬|en}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|北区 (東京都)|en}} to the o11osite bank in {{ill|Adachi-ku|ja|足立区|en}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River|Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja|荒川 (関東)|en}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...
