revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隅田川の渡し]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隅田川の渡し]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隅田川の渡し]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11658843]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Suijin no Watashi|lt=Suijin no Watashi|lt=Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumida River crossings|qq=}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=The Ferry at Fujimi}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|富嶽三十六景|1=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|2=ja|3=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}} and {{ill|総州|en|総州|WD=Q67433108|lt=Sōshū}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1923 Great Kantō earthquake|qq=}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|Car|en|Car|WD=Q1420|lt=automobiles}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Toden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都電車|1=Tokyo Toden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7814005}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|en|Nippon Kayaku|WD=Q7039635|lt=Nippon Kayaku}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku, Tokyo}} to the o11osite bank in {{ill|Adachi, Tokyo|en|Adachi, Tokyo|WD=Q213464|lt=Adachi-ku}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkamiya Bridge|qq=}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjiji Temple|1=Nishiarai Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608171}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|en|Arakawa River (Kantō)|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa River}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒川 (関東)|1=Arakawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q625465}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyabori Children's Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
...

Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|en|Nippon Kayaku|WD=Q7039635|lt=Nippon Kayaku}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku, Tokyo}} to the o11osite bank in {{ill|Adachi, Tokyo|en|Adachi, Tokyo|WD=Q213464|lt=Adachi-ku}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkamiya Bridge|qq=}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjiji Temple|1=Nishiarai Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608171}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|en|Arakawa River (Kantō)|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa River}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒川 (関東)|1=Arakawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q625465}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyabori Children's Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}} and {{ill|総州|en|総州|WD=Q67433108|lt=Sōshū}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1923 Great Kantō earthquake|qq=}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|Car|en|Car|WD=Q1420|lt=automobiles}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Toden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都電車|1=Tokyo Toden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7814005}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|en|Nippon Kayaku|WD=Q7039635|lt=Nippon Kayaku}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku, Tokyo}} to the o11osite bank in {{ill|Adachi, Tokyo|en|Adachi, Tokyo|WD=Q213464|lt=Adachi-ku}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>

The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyabori Children's Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>

{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|lt=Suijin no Watashi|lt=Sumida River crossings|ja|隅田川の渡し|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumida River crossings|qq=}} [[File:Sunset across the Ryogoku bridge from the bank of the Sumida river at Onmagayashi.jpg|thumb|320px|{{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=The Ferry at Fujimi|1=Hokusai}} ("{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja|富嶽三十六景|1=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|2=ja|3=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}")]] [[File:Tsukudajima no watashi old.jpg|thumb|320px|The Ferry at Tsukudajima (early Shōwa period)]]
'''Sumida River Crossings''' (隅田川の渡し, ''Sumida-gawa no Watashi'') were a collection of ferry routes and stations that once operated along the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River|1=Sumida River}}.
For centuries, ferries served as essential transport links on the routes leading to {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province}} and {{ill|総州|en|総州|WD=Q67433108|lt=Sōshū|1=総州}}, particularly before the advent of widespread bridge construction. As Edo grew under the Tokugawa shogunate, the need for crossings increased. However, defensive considerations and maintenance costs limited the number of bridges, necessitating numerous ferries to connect the north and south of Edo's expanding urban area.
Throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the number of crossings steadily increased. By the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, there were over 20 documented ferry routes. However, following the {{ill|1923 Great Kantō earthquake|ja|関東大震災|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1923 Great Kantō earthquake|qq=}}, new bridges capable of accommodating {{ill|Car|en|Car|WD=Q1420|lt=automobiles|1=Car}} and {{ill|Tokyo Toden|ja|東京都電|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Toden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都電車|1=Tokyo Toden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7814005}} trams gradually replaced the ferries. The last official public ferry route, "Shiomi no Watashi," ceased operations in 1966 (Shōwa 41).
Today, the only remaining operational ferry is a private route operated by {{ill|Nippon Kayaku|en|Nippon Kayaku|WD=Q7039635|lt=Nippon Kayaku|1=Nippon Kayaku}} for its employees, connecting its Tokyo factory in {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Kita, Tokyo}} to the o11osite bank in {{ill|Adachi, Tokyo|en|Adachi, Tokyo|WD=Q213464|lt=Adachi-ku|1=Adachi, Tokyo}}. This ferry is not open to the public.<ref>[http://www.mizu.gr.jp/bunkajuku/houkoku/010_20121206_arakawa.html 実施報告 船でゆく荒川-人工水路と暮らしの接点-] - {{ill|Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja|ミツカン 水の文化センター|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizkan Water Culture Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, retrieved February 23, 2017.</ref>
== Major Crossings == Below is a list of notable Sumida River ferry crossings from upstream to downstream. The locations and names of these ferries changed over time, so the representative names and positions are noted.
=== Miyabori no Watashi === Also called "Kamiyabori no Watashi," this ferry operated near the current {{ill|Shinkamiya Bridge|ja|新神谷橋|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkamiya Bridge|qq=}}. It primarily served pilgrims visiting {{ill|Sōjiji Temple|ja|總持寺 (足立区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjiji Temple|1=Nishiarai Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608171}} and flower-viewing visitors to the banks of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|en|Arakawa River (Kantō)|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa River|1=Arakawa River (Kantō)}} during the Edo period. Following the construction of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Kantō)|lt=Arakawa Spillway|ja|荒川放水路|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Kantō)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒川 (関東)|1=Arakawa River (Kantō)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q625465}} in 1924 (Taishō 13), the area became isolated as a mid-river island. The crossing was eventually replaced by Shinkamiya Bridge, completed in 1965 (Shōwa 40). A signboard commemorating the ferry is installed in {{ill|Miyabori Children's Park|ja|宮堀児童遊園|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyabori Children's Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Arakawa Spillway Construction|url=http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/arage/|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|language=ja|en}}</ref>
